◈ Infrastructure Sizing Tool

Dev Cluster Calculator / User Guide

Proxmox · Spark Development Cluster · 3 nodes · 32c / 384 GB / 7× 3.84 TB NVMe · 3× 480 GB SSD per node

Table of Contents
  1. Overview & Purpose
  2. Header — Scenario Management & Hardware Constants
  3. Diagnostics & Warnings
  4. Infrastructure Parameters
  5. Spark Executor Parameters
  6. Fixed Overhead Reservations
  7. Resource Budget
  8. Executor Math
  9. Storage Layout & Spark Configuration
  10. Parquet Partitioning
  11. Network Path Estimator
  12. How Everything Connects — The Calculation Chain

1. Overview & Purpose

This calculator sizes a 3-node Proxmox hyperconverged development cluster running Apache Spark via YARN, Ceph for distributed storage (with S3 access via RGW), and a daily ETL pipeline that reads raw CSV data, converts it to Parquet, stages it to Ceph RGW, then loads it into Snowflake via COPY INTO.

Hardware is fixed: 3 nodes, each with 32 cores (single AMD EPYC socket), 384 GB RAM, 7× 3.84 TB NVMe, and 3× 480 GB SSD (two-drive ZFS mirror + hot spare). The calculator models how those fixed resources are divided between infrastructure services, Spark worker VMs, and Spark executors.

Key difference from production: the development cluster has a single CPU socket per node, which means a maximum of 32 vCPUs per VM with no cross-NUMA traffic. The production cluster has 2 sockets (64 cores per node) where the NUMA boundary constraint is more operationally significant. On dev, NUMA pinning has limited effect since there is only one memory domain per node.

All sliders are live — every change immediately recalculates all outputs, bar charts, metric tiles, and warnings without any page reload or submit step.

2. Header — Scenario Management & Hardware Constants

Save & Reset

Save writes the current slider positions to browser localStorage under the key spark_calc_dev. The next time you open the calculator in the same browser, the session is automatically restored to the saved state. Reset clears that localStorage entry and returns every slider to its factory default value.

Named Scenarios

The scenario system lets you preserve and compare multiple configurations:

  • Save Scenario — prompts for a name, then stores a named snapshot in a separate localStorage key (spark_calc_dev_scenarios). Any number of named scenarios can coexist.
  • Load — applies a saved scenario’s slider values and immediately calls recalc(). Loading a scenario does not overwrite the session continuity save — you must click Save explicitly if you want the session to persist.
  • View JSON — opens a popup showing the raw JSON for the selected scenario. Useful for copying settings to share with a colleague or pasting into a ticket.
  • Delete — removes the named scenario from localStorage. The current slider state is not affected.

Hardware Constants Bar

The constants bar at the bottom of the header displays the fixed physical parameters for the cluster: NODES = 3, CORES/NODE = 32, NUMA DOMAINS = 1 (single socket — this is the primary difference from the production cluster), SSDs/NODE = 3×480 GB (ZFS mirror + hot spare), and NVMe/NODE = 7×3.84 TB. RAM/NODE is adjustable via the infrastructure slider and reflects the current slider value.

Display Theme

The ☼ Light / ☾ Dark button at the right end of the action bar toggles between dark mode (default) and the warm-tone light theme. Your preference is stored in localStorage under the key spark_theme and is restored automatically on your next visit. The setting is shared between the development and production calculators — switching theme in one applies it to both.

3. Diagnostics & Warnings

The Diagnostics card is positioned at the top of the page so configuration errors are immediately visible. It re-evaluates on every slider change. The possible states are:

Indicator Severity Meaning & Remediation
✕ Core overcommit Error VMs are claiming more cores than the node has available after reservations. Reduce vCPUs/VM or VMs/node until the core budget bar shows headroom.
✕ RAM overcommit Error VMs are claiming more RAM than the node has after infrastructure reservations. Reduce RAM/VM. The RAM/VM slider prevents exceeding the hardware ceiling, but changes to OSD count or the services VM can push a previously valid value into overcommit.
✕ Executor OOM Error The calculated executor.memory × (1 + overhead) + offheap exceeds the slot size. YARN will kill the container on startup. Reduce executor cores (to fit more, smaller executors) or reduce the overhead fraction.
⚠ OOM margin very thin Warning The free margin per executor slot is less than 4 GB. Data skew, large Parquet files, or broadcast joins can push native memory over the YARN container limit. Consider reducing off-heap allocation or increasing RAM/VM.
✕ NUMA boundary crossed Error vCPUs per VM exceeds 32. On the development cluster (single socket), this cannot occur physically — the vCPU slider is hard-capped at 32. The check exists for model consistency and activates if the cap is bypassed programmatically.
⚠ NUMA pinning disabled Warning Recommends enabling NUMA pinning for lower memory latency. On dev (single socket), the practical benefit is minimal, but enabling it is still a best practice for consistent memory access patterns.
⚠ AQE disabled Warning Shuffle partitions will be a fixed count. For variable-size daily loads, fixed partitions are often too high (small datasets) or too low (large datasets). Enabling Adaptive Query Execution allows Spark to coalesce partitions at runtime.
⚠ Low executor density Warning Fewer than 3 executors per VM. With only 1–2 executors per VM, a single skewed task can stall the entire node. Aim for at least 3 executors per VM by reducing spark.executor.cores or increasing vCPUs/VM.
✕ Ceph capacity critically low Error Ceph usable capacity divided by daily ingest volume is less than 3 days. Increase OSD count, reduce the replication factor (with caution), or increase NVMe-to-OSD allocation.
⚠ Limited Ceph retention Warning Ceph retention is less than 7 days at the current ingest rate. Consider adding more OSDs or reviewing data lifecycle policies.
⚠ Scratch disk may be insufficient Warning Scratch TB per VM is less than 75% of worst-case shuffle volume (daily_TB × shuffle_amp). Shuffle spill to disk will likely exhaust local scratch. Reduce OSD count to free more NVMe for scratch, or reduce shuffle amplification.
✓ Configuration looks healthy OK All checks pass. No overcommit, adequate OOM margin, sufficient Ceph retention, adequate scratch space, and healthy executor density.

4. Infrastructure Parameters

These sliders define the physical and virtual machine topology. They feed the reservation math and set the ceiling for all executor calculations. Every change here propagates immediately through the entire calculation chain.

Slider Range What it controls & what it affects
RAM per node (GB) 128–768 GB, step 32 One step equals one 32 GB DIMM. Sets the total memory budget for the node. Dynamically recalculates the RAM/VM slider’s maximum ceiling every time it moves. At 256 GB (8 DIMMs) the dev cluster operates at 66.6% of peak memory bandwidth per HPE DDR5 DIMM population rules. Increasing toward 384 GB improves bandwidth utilization.
VMs per node 1–2 How many Spark worker VMs share each node’s resources. 1 VM gets the full single socket with all available cores and RAM. 2 VMs each get half the available cores and half the RAM. Also halves the scratch NVMe allocation per VM. Use 2 VMs to isolate different workloads or Spark versions.
vCPUs per worker VM 8–32, step 2 Maximum is 32 — the single-socket boundary for this cluster. The usable vCPU count for executors is this value minus 2 (reserved for guest OS + YARN NodeManager). Directly drives EXEC_PER_VM = floor((vCPUs−2) ÷ exec_cores) and feeds the core budget bar.
RAM per worker VM (GB) 64 GB to dynamic max, step 32 The dynamic maximum is computed as floor((RAM_NODE − reservations) ÷ VMs_per_node / 32) × 32. You cannot set this higher than the hardware allows — the slider ceiling auto-adjusts whenever RAM/node, OSD count, or the services VM toggle changes. Usable RAM for executors = RAM_VM − 2 GB (YARN NodeManager overhead).
NVMe drives → Ceph OSDs 0–6, step 1 Splits the 7 NVMe drives between Ceph distributed storage and local Spark scratch. Each OSD contributes 3.84 TB to the Ceph raw pool and reserves 1 core + 3 GB RAM from the node budget. The remaining drives (7 − OSD_count) are divided across worker VMs as scratch space for shuffle spill. Setting to 0 disables Ceph storage entirely.
Ceph replication factor 2–3× Determines how many copies of each data block Ceph maintains. Ceph usable = raw ÷ rep_factor. 3× means 3 copies of every byte — safe against 2 simultaneous drive failures but uses 3× the raw capacity. 2× recovers more usable space but tolerates only 1 failure. Only affects storage capacity and days-of-ingest metrics — no CPU or RAM impact.
NUMA pinning on/off toggle On the development cluster (single socket per node), NUMA pinning has limited operational benefit since there is only one memory domain. The toggle controls warning text — disabling it triggers a yellow advisory. It does not change any numeric calculation. Enable it as a best practice to ensure consistent memory access patterns and to match production behavior.
AQE enabled on/off toggle Adaptive Query Execution (spark.sql.adaptive.enabled). When ON: spark.sql.shuffle.partitions is treated as a ceiling and Spark coalesces small partitions at runtime based on actual data sizes — critical for variable ETL loads. When OFF: the calculated partition count is used as a fixed value, which is often too high for small datasets and too low for skewed ones. Disabling triggers a yellow warning.
YARN services VM on/off toggle Enables a dedicated VM for YARN ResourceManager, History Server, Hive Metastore, PostgreSQL, and Airflow. Its core and RAM cost is amortized across all 3 nodes: ceil(SVC_cores ÷ 3) cores and ceil(SVC_RAM ÷ 3) GB RAM are deducted from each node’s available budget. Enabling this reduces the ceiling for the RAM/VM and vCPUs/VM sliders.
Services VM vCPUs 4–8, step 2 Only visible when the YARN services VM toggle is on. Deducted from available cores across all nodes (÷ 3 per node). Higher values leave less headroom for worker VMs but reduce resource contention on the services VM during heavy Hive or Airflow activity.
Services VM RAM (GB) 8–24 GB, step 4 Only visible when the YARN services VM toggle is on. Deducted from available RAM across all nodes (÷ 3 per node). Also shrinks the RAM/VM slider ceiling. Metastore and Airflow benefit significantly from 16–20 GB; 8 GB is the minimum for stable operation.
Dynamic RAM/VM ceiling: The RAM/VM slider is the only slider whose range changes dynamically. Its maximum is always derived from the current RAM/node, OSD count, services VM settings, and VMs/node values — you can never accidentally over-allocate memory through the slider UI. If an existing value exceeds the new ceiling after a topology change, the slider is proportionally rescaled to maintain the same percentage position.

5. Spark Executor Parameters

These sliders control how Spark executors are sized within each worker VM and how the shuffle partition strategy is calculated. The executor parameters directly determine job throughput, GC pressure, and YARN container stability.

Slider Range What it controls & what it affects
spark.executor.cores 2–10, step 1 Controls how many vCPUs each executor uses. Fewer cores = more executors per VM = smaller memory slot each (faster GC, lower heap pressure). More cores = fewer executors = larger memory slot (better for large joins). The current default of 4 gives a good balance between executor count and heap size on this cluster’s RAM-per-core ratio — enough parallelism for concurrent S3 reads/writes without oversized heaps that stress GC. Directly drives EXEC_PER_VM = floor((vCPUs−2) ÷ exec_cores).
Overhead fraction (%) 8–20%, step 1 Maps to spark.executor.memoryOverheadFactor. The JVM reserves this fraction of executor heap for native structures: thread stacks, NIO buffers, and internal metadata. If set too low, YARN kills the container when native memory exceeds the container limit — this appears as a sudden executor loss without a Java exception. Formula: MAX_EXEC_MEM = (SLOT_GB − offheap_GB) ÷ (1 + overhead_fraction).
Off-heap size (GB) 0–16 GB, step 2 Maps to spark.memory.offHeap.size. Carves a fixed GB out of the executor slot for Spark’s vectorized Parquet reader and serialization buffers — this memory lives outside the JVM heap and is not garbage-collected. Every GB here directly reduces the heap available to executor.memory. Set to 0 to disable off-heap entirely (spark.memory.offHeap.enabled = false).
Daily data volume (TB) 0.5–4 TB, step 0.5 Total data ingested per day across all batches/dumps. Drives: Ceph days-of-ingest calculation, scratch adequacy warning threshold (daily_TB × shuffle_amp), and spark.sql.shuffle.partitions estimate. Note: distinct from CSV dataset size (set adjacent to this slider in Workload Sizing), which represents one batch and drives Parquet partitioning math.
Shuffle amplification 1–4×, step 0.5 How much data expands during shuffle operations (groupBy, join, sort). A simple filter amplifies ~1×. A join between two equal-sized tables approaches 2×. Multi-stage pipelines with skewed keys can reach 3–4×. The current default of 3.5× reflects a typical ETL pipeline with mixed broadcast and sort-merge joins and moderate key skew. Drives: SHUFFLE_DATA_TB = daily_TB × amp → CALC_PARTITIONS → SHUFFLE_PARTS, and sets the denominator for the scratch warning threshold.
Shuffle output size (MB) 64–512 MB, step 32 Maps to spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes. After joins and aggregations produce many small shuffle partitions, AQE coalesces them back toward this target size. This is the output-side / post-shuffle control — it has no effect on how CSV files are read. CALC_PARTITIONS = ceil(SHUFFLE_DATA_MB ÷ target_MB). Smaller value = more post-shuffle tasks = more parallelism but higher scheduling overhead. Larger value = fewer tasks = less overhead. Entirely independent of Read chunk size, which controls the input read phase only.
SLOT_GB = (RAM_VM − 2) ÷ EXEC_PER_VM
MAX_EXEC_MEM = (SLOT_GB − offheap_GB) ÷ (1 + overhead_fraction)
EXEC_MEM = floor(MAX_EXEC_MEM × 0.95) // 5% safety margin
OOM_MARGIN = SLOT_GB − (EXEC_MEM × (1 + overhead) + offheap)
Understanding executors and off-heap memory

When Spark runs a job, YARN launches executor processes on your worker VMs — one or more per VM. Each executor is a self-contained JVM (Java Virtual Machine) process that gets its own slice of RAM and CPU cores. Tasks — the individual units of computation (“process partition 47 of the CSV”) — are handed to executors, which run them in parallel across their allocated cores. 

Worker VM  (e.g. 384 GB RAM, 32 vCPUs)
├── Executor 1  (e.g. 120 GB RAM, 5 cores)  ← JVM process
├── Executor 2  (e.g. 120 GB RAM, 5 cores)  ← JVM process
└── OS + NodeManager overhead

Each executor runs tasks concurrently — one per core — so a 5-core executor runs 5 tasks simultaneously.

Two separate memory regions inside every executor 

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     EXECUTOR PROCESS                        │
│                                                             │
│  ┌───────────────────────┐   ┌───────────────────────────┐  │
│  │      JVM HEAP         │   │    OFF-HEAP (native)      │  │
│  │  spark.executor       │   │    spark.memory           │  │
│  │  .memory              │   │    .offHeap.size          │  │
│  │                       │   │                           │  │
│  │  • Execution memory   │   │  • Parquet column         │  │
│  │    (shuffles, joins,  │   │    buffers                │  │
│  │    sort, aggregation) │   │  • Arrow columnar         │  │
│  │  • Storage memory     │   │    encoding / decoding    │  │
│  │    (cached datasets)  │   │  • Decompression          │  │
│  │  • User code objects  │   │    scratch space          │  │
│  │                       │   │                           │  │
│  │  Managed by GC ✓      │   │  No GC — direct OS ✓     │  │
│  └───────────────────────┘   └───────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                             │
│  + JVM overhead (thread stacks, NIO buffers, JVM internals) │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

The JVM heap is where Java objects live — DataFrames being processed, shuffle buffers, cached datasets. The JVM’s garbage collector (GC) automatically frees unused objects. GC is a blessing (no manual memory management) and a burden: when it runs, it pauses all threads in the executor. On a heap loaded with large byte arrays — like a 3 GB Parquet column chunk — GC pauses can last seconds, stalling tasks and triggering YARN timeouts.

Off-heap memory is allocated directly from the OS, bypassing the JVM entirely. The GC never sees it, never touches it, never pauses for it.

Why Parquet and Arrow specifically need off-heap

When Spark reads a Parquet file it works through three stages: 

1. Read       — compressed column chunks arrive from disk or S3 / Ceph into a raw byte buffer
2. Decompress — Snappy / Zstd expand the bytes into raw column data
3. Decode     — raw bytes are converted into values Spark can operate on (ints, strings, timestamps)

These intermediate buffers can be gigabytes per column for wide tables. Placing them on the JVM heap would constantly trigger GC. Apache Arrow (the in-memory columnar format Spark 3.x uses internally) allocates these buffers off-heap — no GC pause when a 2 GB column buffer is freed, and native vectorized decoders written in C++ can operate directly on the contiguous memory.

The silent failure mode: setting off-heap to 0 while Spark’s vectorized reader still tries to allocate native buffers can cause container-kill OOM crashes that produce no Java stack trace — the OS kills the process before the JVM can catch the error. The symptom is an executor that disappears from the YARN UI with no exception logged.
WorkloadOff-heap recommendation
Reading / writing Parquet or ORC2–8 GB depending on column width and file sizes
Heavy joins, window functionsErr toward more — large Arrow buffers during wide aggregations
Pure RDD operations (no structured API)0 — off-heap is never used
In-memory DataFrame ops, no file I/O0 or 1–2 GB as a safety margin
CSV → Parquet → Snowflake ETL (this project)4 GB — starting point for 2 TB/day at current scale

Executor Core Sensitivity

Below the executor parameter sliders, the Executor Core Sensitivity card shows a three-column comparison of how key metrics change if you use one fewer or one more core per executor than the current setting. It answers the question: what do I actually gain or lose by moving the exec_cores slider one notch?

The table always shows three scenarios side by side:

  • 4C −1 — one fewer core per executor than current
  • 5C ★ Current — your current exec_cores setting (highlighted column)
  • 6C +1 — one more core per executor than current

Each row is colour-coded using the same palette as the Operating Envelope card:

  • ● Safe — value is within healthy operating range
  • ● Marginal — value is within bounds but approaching a threshold
  • ● Unsafe — value violates a threshold (OOM risk, overcommit, etc.)
  • ● Informational — metric has no threshold; shown for reference only

A colour legend is displayed below the table for quick reference. The current column is highlighted with a light background. Non-current cells use the default text colour (no dimming) so all three scenarios remain readable.

Use this card to quickly evaluate trade-offs before committing to a slider change: fewer cores per executor gives more executors (more task parallelism, smaller heaps, lower GC risk); more cores gives fewer executors with larger memory slots (better for wide joins and large aggregations).

6. Fixed Overhead Reservations

Before any Spark worker VM can be sized, a block of cores and RAM is reserved on every node for infrastructure services. These resources are invisible to Spark — they never appear in YARN’s resource pool and cannot be allocated to executors.

Service Cores reserved RAM reserved Notes
Proxmox hypervisor 2c 8 GB Fixed. Host OS kernel, QEMU process overhead, and live-migration buffers. Cannot be reduced.
Ceph OSDs (N×) 1c per OSD 3 GB per OSD Scales with the NVMe→OSDs slider. The 3 GB covers BlueStore WAL, RocksDB metadata cache, and OS page cache pressure per OSD process.
Ceph RGW (S3 gateway) 4c 8 GB Fixed. 4 frontend worker threads serving the S3 API to Spark (for shuffle remote reads) and to Snowflake’s COPY INTO command.
Ceph MON (monitor) 2c 6 GB Fixed. One monitor daemon per node participating in the Paxos quorum for cluster state management.
YARN services VM ceil(SVC_cores ÷ 3) ceil(SVC_RAM ÷ 3) GB Only shown when the services VM toggle is enabled. Cost is amortized: one-third of the total services VM size is deducted from each node.
TOTAL RESERVED sum of above sum of above Displayed in the overhead card. Varies with OSD count and services VM settings.
Available for VMs CORES_NODE − reserved RAM_NODE − reserved The pool from which worker VM allocations are drawn. Shown in the overhead card as the final row.
Effective max / VM available ÷ VMs_per_node available ÷ VMs_per_node Hard ceiling for the vCPUs/VM and RAM/VM sliders. The RAM/VM slider enforces this ceiling dynamically.
Ceiling effect: The “Effective max / VM” row is the hard ceiling for the vCPUs/VM and RAM/VM sliders. If you increase OSD count or enable the services VM, this ceiling drops and the RAM/VM slider will automatically clamp down to the new maximum — your previously configured RAM/VM value may silently decrease. Always check the overhead card after changing storage topology.

7. Resource Budget

The Resource Budget card provides a visual summary of how cores and RAM are consumed across the four budget categories on each node. Both bars update in real time on every slider change.

Core Budget Bar

The core bar is divided into four segments: Proxmox (fixed dark segment, always 2 cores) → Ceph + RGW (amber, scales with OSD count: 4 RGW + 2 MON + 1×OSDs) → Worker VMs (blue, vCPUs/VM × VMs/node) → Free headroom (green if positive, red if overcommit). The free/overcommit segment is what the Diagnostics card monitors for the core overcommit error.

RAM Budget Bar

Same four-segment structure for RAM: Proxmox (8 GB) → Ceph + RGW (8 GB RGW + 6 GB MON + 3 GB×OSDs) → Worker VMs (RAM/VM × VMs/node) → Free (green) or Overcommit (red). Services VM reservation is included in the Ceph+RGW segment when enabled.

Metric Tiles

Four tiles below the bars provide quick-reference values:

  • Free cores: cores not allocated to any reservation or VM. Green = headroom, red = overcommit.
  • Free RAM: RAM not allocated. Green = headroom, red = overcommit. Both of these values feed the Diagnostics overcommit checks.
  • Executors/VM: how many Spark executors fit in one worker VM given the current vCPUs/VM and exec_cores settings.
  • Total executors: (executors/VM × total VMs) − 1. The −1 reserves one executor slot for the YARN cluster-mode application driver.
Overcommit indicator: Free cores/RAM tiles turning red means you have over-allocated. Spark will not be able to acquire YARN containers and jobs will queue indefinitely or fail. The Diagnostics card at the top will also flag this with a ✕ error. Reduce vCPUs/VM, RAM/VM, or the number of VMs per node to restore headroom.

8. Executor Math

The Executor Math card shows the step-by-step memory breakdown for a single executor slot and the resulting configuration values that are written to spark-defaults.conf. Every value here is derived from the Infrastructure and Executor Parameter sliders.

Memory Breakdown Bar

The horizontal bar shows how one executor slot’s RAM is divided into four segments:

  • Executor heap (blue) — spark.executor.memory: the JVM heap Spark requests from YARN. This is what most Spark tuning guides refer to as “executor memory.”
  • JVM overhead (amber) — overhead_fraction × executor.memory: reserved for thread stacks, NIO buffers, and native JVM structures. YARN enforces this as a hard container limit.
  • Off-heap (purple) — spark.memory.offHeap.size: vectorized Parquet read buffers outside the GC-managed heap. Zero when off-heap is disabled.
  • OOM margin (green if ≥4 GB, yellow if 0–4 GB, red if negative) — the free space between the sum of the above three segments and the slot ceiling. A negative margin means the executor will OOM on startup.

Metric Tiles

  • Slot size (GB): (RAM_VM − 2) ÷ EXEC_PER_VM — the RAM envelope available to one executor, including all overhead components.
  • executor.memory: the heap value written to spark-defaults.conf. This is what YARN sees as the memory request when launching the container.
  • OOM risk: LOW (green, margin ≥ 4 GB) / MARGINAL (yellow, margin 0–4 GB) / HIGH (red, negative margin — job will crash on container startup). The 4 GB threshold accounts for typical data skew and broadcast join overhead.
  • Executors/VM: floor((vCPUs − 2) ÷ exec_cores) — how many executors fit given the core budget. The −2 reserves vCPUs for the guest OS and YARN NodeManager daemon.
  • Total executors: (executors/VM × total VMs) − 1 — the −1 reserves one executor for the YARN cluster-mode application driver. This is the value written to spark.executor.instances.
  • Total executor cores: TOTAL_EXEC × exec_cores — feeds spark.default.parallelism = total_exec_cores × 2.

9. Storage Layout & Spark Configuration

Storage Layout

The storage card provides a visual map of how drives are allocated on each node. The layout is fixed for the SSD tier and configurable for NVMe:

  • 3× SSD (480 GB each): two drives form the Proxmox OS ZFS mirror; the third is configured as a ZFS hot spare. All three are never available to Ceph or Spark.
  • NVMe OSDs (amber): drives assigned to Ceph via the NVMe→OSDs slider. Each contributes 3.84 TB to the Ceph raw pool across all 3 nodes. Total raw = OSD_count × 3.84 TB × 3 nodes.
  • NVMe scratch (green): remaining NVMe drives (7 − OSD_count) divided across worker VMs. SCRATCH_TB_PER_VM = (NVME_COUNT − OSD_count) ÷ VMs_per_node × 3.84 TB. Used as spark.local.dir for shuffle spill and intermediate sort output.

The three metric tiles below the drive map show: Ceph usable TB (raw ÷ replication factor), days of ingest at the current daily volume, and scratch TB per VM.

Spark Configuration Table

The Spark config table is auto-generated from the current slider values. Every computed row updates when any slider changes. The table is ready to copy directly into spark-defaults.conf. Key properties:

Property Source
spark.executor.cores Directly from the exec_cores slider.
spark.executor.memory The calculated EXEC_MEM value in GB with the “g” suffix. This is the heap Spark requests from YARN.
spark.executor.memoryOverheadFactor Overhead fraction slider ÷ 100. YARN adds this fraction on top of executor.memory for native overhead.
spark.memory.offHeap.enabled / .size false / 0g when the off-heap slider is 0. true / Ng otherwise. Off-heap memory is not garbage-collected.
spark.executor.instances TOTAL_EXEC = (executors/VM × total VMs) − 1 for YARN cluster-mode driver.
spark.default.parallelism TOTAL_EXEC × exec_cores × 2. The ×2 accounts for task pipelining and prevents underutilization when some tasks finish early.
spark.sql.shuffle.partitions Calculated from SHUFFLE_DATA_MB ÷ target_partition_size_MB. Capped at a reasonable minimum. When AQE is enabled, Spark coalesces small partitions at runtime.
spark.sql.adaptive.* (3 rows) All three AQE properties reflect the AQE toggle: spark.sql.adaptive.enabled, coalescePartitions.enabled, and skewJoin.enabled.
spark.sql.parquet.compression.codec Fixed to snappy. Snappy is the Snowflake-compatible codec — changing this may cause COPY INTO to fail or produce suboptimal load performance.
spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.endpoint Points to the local Ceph RGW S3 endpoint. Must be set for Spark to find your S3 staging bucket instead of routing to AWS. Also requires path.style.access = true for non-AWS S3 endpoints.
spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes From the Read chunk size slider (Workload Sizing). Spark splits your CSV batch into chunks of at most this size — one parallel read task per chunk. Smaller = more read tasks = more parallelism; larger = fewer tasks = less scheduling overhead. Has no effect on shuffle output.
df.repartition(N) Code-level recommendation for output Parquet partition count. Not a spark-defaults property — must be called explicitly in the ETL job before writing to Ceph RGW.

10. Parquet Partitioning

This card models the transformation from raw CSV input to staged Parquet files in Ceph RGW. It determines how many parallel read tasks Spark launches (input partitions), how many output files are written to the staging bucket (output partitions), and whether those files fall in Snowflake’s optimal COPY INTO range. The sliders here are independent from the daily volume slider in the Executor Parameters section.

Slider Range What it controls & what it affects
CSV dataset size 1–10,000 GB, step 1 GB (auto-formats as GB / TB) The total size of one CSV batch/dump. Now set in Workload Sizing, adjacent to Daily data volume. The two sliders are related but distinct: Daily data volume is the cumulative total ingested per day (used for Ceph and scratch sizing); CSV dataset size is the size of a single job’s input (used for Parquet partitioning math). They are equal only when there is one dump per day. Default 3 GB reflects the current development workload (~2.5 GB rounded to nearest GB).
Parquet compression ratio 2×–10×, step 1 How much smaller the Parquet output is vs the CSV input, due to columnar encoding and Snappy compression. Typical business data compresses 4×–6×. Highly repetitive data (many NULL columns, low-cardinality categoricals) can reach 8×–10×. PARQ_MB = CSV_MB ÷ ratio. Higher ratio = smaller files = faster network transfer to Snowflake but potentially fewer output partitions, which may fall below the Snowflake sweet spot.
Read chunk size 64–256 MB, step 32 Maps to spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes. Spark cannot hand an entire CSV file to one executor — it splits the file into chunks so multiple executors can read in parallel. This slider sets the maximum chunk size; one read task is spawned per chunk. INPUT_PARTS = ceil(CSV_MB ÷ chunk_MB). Smaller values = more tasks = more parallelism at the cost of scheduling overhead. Larger values = fewer tasks = less overhead. This is an input-side / read-phase control only — it has no effect on shuffle output. Entirely independent of Shuffle output size.
How Read chunk size and Shuffle output size relate: These two sliders look similar but govern completely different stages of a Spark job.

Read chunk size (spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes) is the input-side control. Spark splits your CSV batch into chunks of at most this size and spawns one read task per chunk. For example, a 2,500 MB CSV with a 128 MB read chunk size produces 20 parallel read tasks. It has no effect on what happens after the shuffle.

Shuffle output size (spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes) is the output-side / post-shuffle control. After joins and aggregations produce many small shuffle partitions, AQE coalesces them back toward this target size. It has no effect on the read phase. 

CSV files → [Read chunk size] → read tasks → shuffle → [Shuffle output size via AQE] → output tasks

Both default to 128 MB, which is why they appear identical — but they solve different problems at different points in job execution. Tune them independently: Read chunk size when read parallelism is the concern; Shuffle output size when post-shuffle task count or output file size is the concern.

Output Metrics

  • Parquet size: PARQ_MB = CSV_MB ÷ compression — the data volume that travels across the network from Ceph RGW to Snowflake on Azure. This is the value used in the Network Path Estimator transfer time calculation.
  • Input partitions: ceil(CSV_MB ÷ max_part_MB) — how many parallel Spark tasks are spawned to read the CSV. Should be at least equal to total executor cores for full parallelism.
  • Output partitions: ceil(PARQ_MB ÷ 150 MB) — the recommended df.repartition(N) value that produces ~150 MB Parquet files in Ceph RGW. This is the value shown in the Spark config table as the code-level recommendation.
  • Output file size: colour-coded. Green if 64–300 MB (Snowflake sweet spot). Yellow if outside that range, indicating potential COPY INTO inefficiency.
Snowflake COPY INTO file size guidance: Snowflake performs best with files between 100–256 MB. Files below 64 MB create excessive metadata overhead and increase the number of micro-partitions Snowflake must manage. Files above 300 MB reduce Snowflake’s ability to parallelize the load across warehouse threads. The output file size tile turns yellow when outside this range, and the output partitions count in the Spark config table adjusts accordingly.

11. Network Path Estimator

This card models the three-segment network path from Ceph RGW to Snowflake on Azure. The bottleneck is always the slowest segment, and all timing calculations use 80% of bottleneck capacity as the effective throughput to model realistic sustained utilization under TCP overhead and competing traffic.

Important: Ceph RGW must have a publicly reachable S3 endpoint for Snowflake to execute COPY INTO directly. Snowflake pulls the data from your S3-compatible endpoint — it does not receive a push from the Spark job. The ETL pattern is: Spark writes Parquet to Ceph RGW → job completes → Snowflake task runs COPY INTO my_table FROM @my_ceph_stage → Snowflake reads the files over the public internet path modeled here.

Slider Range What it controls & what it affects
Node → Switch (Gbps) 10–100 Gbps, step 10 Per-node NIC speed northbound to the top-of-rack switch. The calculator aggregates all 3 nodes: AGG = speed × 3. So 10 Gbps/node = 30 Gbps aggregate. If the aggregate node bandwidth exceeds the switch uplink capacity, the switch becomes the bottleneck even before traffic reaches the WAN.
Switch → Core (Gbps) 10–100 Gbps, step 10 The switch uplink to the core/border router. If lower than the aggregate node capacity, outbound traffic is queued and throttled at this segment. This is a common bottleneck in on-premises environments where ToR switches have 40 Gbps uplinks serving 4× 10 Gbps server ports.
Core → Snowflake (Mbps) 100–10,000 Mbps, step 100 The WAN or internet circuit speed to Azure where Snowflake is hosted. Displayed in Mbps for precision at sub-1 Gbps values. Converted internally: NET_WAN_G = Mbps ÷ 1000. This segment is almost always the bottleneck for cloud-destined traffic in on-premises deployments. A 1 Gbps (1,000 Mbps) circuit is the default and typical for co-location or dedicated internet access.

Path Diagram

Each segment in the diagram shows its effective speed (aggregate bandwidth for the Node→Switch segment). The bottleneck segment is highlighted in red; non-bottleneck segments are highlighted in green. This makes it immediately obvious which infrastructure investment would have the greatest impact on transfer times.

Output Metrics

  • Bottleneck: identifies which of the three segments is the constraining factor and why (e.g., “WAN circuit is slower than switch uplink”).
  • Eff. throughput: BOTTLENECK_G × 0.80 — realistic sustained rate at 80% utilization, accounting for TCP handshaking, retransmission overhead, and background traffic.
  • Transfer time: PARQ_GB ÷ (EFF_GBPS ÷ 8) — time to move one full daily Parquet load from Ceph RGW to Snowflake. Uses the Parquet size from the Partitioning card (not the raw CSV size).
  • Re-run window: transfer_time × 2 — worst-case time budget if a job fails and must be re-run. Used in SLA planning: if the re-run window exceeds the acceptable ETL window, consider increasing WAN bandwidth or reducing daily data volume.
Default workload sizing: At the default 1,000 Mbps WAN link with a 2,500 MB CSV (approximately 500 MB Parquet at 5× compression), the transfer takes approximately 4–5 seconds at 80% utilization. The 1 Gbps link is fully adequate for this workload — a 10 Gbps WAN upgrade would provide no measurable benefit at current data volumes. The bottleneck would shift to the WAN only if daily CSV input exceeded ~40 GB at 5× compression.

12. How Everything Connects — The Calculation Chain

Every slider feeds into a deterministic calculation chain. Understanding the chain helps you predict the effect of any change before making it. Here is a worked example tracing what happens when you increase spark.executor.cores from 4 (current default) to 5:

Example: Increasing exec_cores from 4 to 5

1. EXEC_PER_VM = floor((vCPUs − 2) ÷ 7) // fewer executors per VM than at ÷5
2. TOTAL_EXEC = (EXEC_PER_VM × total_VMs) − 1 // fewer total executors cluster-wide
3. SLOT_GB = (RAM_VM − 2) ÷ EXEC_PER_VM // slot grows because fewer executors share VM RAM
4. EXEC_MEM = floor(((SLOT_GB − offheap) ÷ (1 + overhead)) × 0.95) // larger heap per executor
5. OOM_MARGIN = SLOT_GB − (EXEC_MEM × (1 + overhead) + offheap) // margin widens → OOM risk improves
6. DEFAULT_PAR = TOTAL_EXEC × 7 × 2 // spark.default.parallelism changes
7. SHUFFLE_PARTS recalculates from daily_TB and target_partition_size // independent of exec_cores
8. Warnings re-evaluate: low executor density warning may appear if EXEC_PER_VM drops below 3

The net effect: each executor gets more cores and more memory, making it better suited to large joins and wide aggregations, but there are fewer executors total so less task-level parallelism. The current default of exec_cores = 4 is well-suited to the dev ETL workload (CSV → Parquet). Increase to 5 if you observe task-level bottlenecks from insufficient core parallelism; increase further only if you see significant GC overhead from very large heaps.

The RAM/VM Pre-Scale Chain

There is one additional cascade that runs before the main calculation: the RAM/VM pre-scale block. It fires whenever any slider changes and must run first because it modifies another slider’s range:

  1. User moves the RAM/node slider from 256 GB to 384 GB.
  2. The pre-scale block runs immediately (before any input values are read): it computes the new RAM/VM max = floor((384 − reservations) ÷ VMs_per_node / 32) × 32.
  3. The current RAM/VM value is proportionally rescaled: new_val = round(current_pct × new_max / 32) × 32, ensuring the slider stays at the same relative position even though the range has changed.
  4. All downstream executor math (SLOT_GB, EXEC_MEM, OOM_MARGIN, everything in the Executor Math card) recalculates with the new RAM/VM value.
  5. The Warnings card re-evaluates all checks with the updated values.

This ordering is why state restoration (loading a saved scenario) must restore ram_node before iterating through other range inputs — if ram_per_vm is restored before ram_node sets the correct ceiling, the RAM/VM slider may clamp to the wrong maximum and the restored configuration will differ from the saved one.

Healthy configuration checklist: A well-tuned configuration shows all green in Diagnostics & Warnings: no core or RAM overcommit — OOM margin ≥ 4 GB per executor slot — Ceph retention ≥ 7 days at current daily ingest volume — scratch TB per VM ≥ 75% of worst-case shuffle volume (daily_TB × shuffle_amp) — at least 3 executors per VM for task-level isolation — AQE enabled for adaptive partition coalescing — NUMA pinning enabled (even on single-socket dev hardware, for consistency with production).
